questions.question: In the context of software security, what is a 'policy'?
options: A concrete implementation that enforces rules
A specification of good (secure) or bad (insecure) software behavior
A programming language feature for access control
A hardware component that monitors system calls
questions.explanation: A policy defines the 'law' — it specifies what behaviors are considered secure or insecure. The mechanism is the concrete enforcer.

questions.question: What is the relationship between a mechanism and a policy?
questions.explanation: A mechanism is the concrete implementation that ensures software adheres to (enforces) a policy.

questions.question: What are the two-word summaries of software security given in the course?
questions.explanation: The two key two-word summaries are 'Minimize trust' and 'Validate inputs' — foundational principles of secure coding.

questions.question: According to the course, what is described as 'the biggest problem in security in computer science'?
options: The gap between high-level and low-level policies
The lack of secure programming languages
questions.explanation: The gap between high-level policies (what we want) and low-level policies (what we can implement) is identified as the biggest problem in security.

questions.question: What is a trace in the context of program security?
questions.explanation: A trace is a sequence of security-relevant actions delimited by semicolons. A program is the set of all possible traces it may exhibit.

questions.question: What does it mean for a program to be 'diverging'?
options: It is non-terminating (produces an infinite trace)
questions.explanation: A diverging program is non-terminating — it produces an infinite trace. A converging program terminates (finite trace).

questions.question: The empty trace (ε) represents a program doing nothing at all.
questions.explanation: The empty trace represents a program doing no security-relevant actions. The program may still be doing work — just nothing that is security-relevant.

questions.question: A trace is a prefix of itself.
questions.explanation: By definition, a ≤ a — every trace is a prefix of itself. Also, the empty trace ε is a prefix of all traces.

questions.question: The empty trace is denoted by the symbol λ (lambda).
questions.explanation: The empty trace is denoted by ε (epsilon), NOT λ (lambda). This is a common misconception noted in the course.

questions.question: A program P can be formally defined as:
options: The set of all possible traces it may exhibit
A mapping from inputs to outputs
questions.explanation: A program is defined as the set of all possible traces it may exhibit. This set-based definition is key to formal security reasoning.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a policy and a mechanism, and give one example of each.
questions.answer: A policy is a specification of secure/insecure behavior (e.g., 'no unauthorized access to files'). A mechanism is a concrete enforcer of a policy (e.g., file permissions, firewalls, or access control lists).

questions.question: What does the empty trace ε represent, and why is this distinction important?
questions.answer: The empty trace ε represents a program performing no security-relevant actions — not that it does nothing. This matters because a program might be busy computing but if none of its actions are security-relevant, its trace is empty.

questions.question: A company's security team writes a document stating 'All customer data must be encrypted at rest and in transit.' An engineer then implements TLS for network traffic and AES-256 for database storage. In formal terms, what did each party produce?
options: The team wrote a mechanism; the engineer created a policy
The team wrote a policy; the engineer created mechanisms
Both created policies at different abstraction levels
Both created mechanisms targeting different threat vectors
questions.explanation: The security document is the policy (specification of desired behavior). TLS and AES-256 implementations are mechanisms (concrete enforcers of that policy).

questions.question: Consider a program that reads a file, processes its contents in memory, and writes output. If the security policy only concerns file access, which parts of the execution appear in the trace?
options: Only the file read and file write operations
The entire execution trace including CPU cycles
questions.explanation: Traces only contain security-relevant actions. If the policy only concerns file access, only the file read and write are security-relevant — memory processing is invisible to the trace.

questions.question: A single mechanism can only enforce one policy.
questions.explanation: There is a many-to-many relationship between mechanisms and policies. A single mechanism can enforce multiple policies, and a single policy can be enforced by multiple mechanisms.

questions.question: If trace t₁ = 'read; write' and trace t₂ = 'read; write; delete', which statement is true?
options: t₂ is a prefix of t₁
t₁ is a prefix of t₂
Neither is a prefix of the other
questions.explanation: t₁ ≤ t₂ because t₁ (read; write) is a prefix of t₂ (read; write; delete). A prefix is any initial subsequence of a trace.

questions.question: An online store has a policy: 'Never charge a customer twice for the same order.' The payment system processes 1000 transactions per second. Is this policy best enforced by a static or dynamic mechanism, and why?
options: Static — analyze the source code for duplicate charge bugs
Dynamic — a runtime monitor can check each transaction against previous ones in real-time
Neither — this policy cannot be enforced
questions.explanation: This requires runtime monitoring of actual transaction data — you can't determine at compile time whether duplicate charges will occur. A dynamic mechanism checking each transaction is appropriate.

questions.question: A program has traces: {read; write; close}, {read; close}, {read; write; write; close}. How many traces does this program have?
questions.explanation: The program is defined as the SET of all its possible traces. This set contains exactly 3 distinct traces.

questions.question: When mechanism M ensures software obeys policy P, we say M _____ P.
questions.explanation: The formal terminology is that mechanism M 'enforces' policy P — it ensures the software adheres to the policy.

questions.question: A converging program produces an infinite trace.
questions.explanation: A converging program is terminating and produces a finite trace. A diverging program is non-terminating and produces an infinite trace.
